READ-ONLY evidence-gathering for defect D86 (H2: liveness DownAfter is not tunable, so a flappy-but-usable primary thrashes the bond). Gather numbered evidence against the ACTUAL repo (codegraph_explore / Read / Grep) — each item a file:line + a 3-5 line excerpt + a one-line relevance note — then RETURN the evidence-json block. Write NOTHING; do not adjudicate.

HYPOTHESIS (id H-D86): the per-path liveness DownAfter (and the probe interval / up-hysteresis) is HARDCODED via telemetry.Default* with NO TOML config knob, so an operator cannot lengthen it or add a brief-silence ride-through to stop a flappy-but-usable primary (Starlink's ~1.2-1.3s micro-outages) from failing the bond over to metered 5G on every blip.

VALIDATE against source (cite file:line):
1. internal/telemetry/liveness.go — read LivenessConfig + the Default* constants (DefaultDownAfter, DefaultProbeInterval, DefaultUpSuccesses). Confirm the values (D16/T39 set DownAfter=1200ms, interval=200ms, up=3) and that these are package constants, not config-derived.
2. How the daemon builds its LivenessConfig: internal/device/device.go — does it read DownAfter/interval FROM config (internal/config) or hardcode telemetry.Default*? Trace the ProberConfig/LivenessConfig construction. Grep internal/config for ANY probe / liveness / down / silence / interval / hysteresis / dwell key — confirm ABSENCE of a TOML knob.
3. EXISTING hysteresis: liveness.go already has an up/down asymmetry (UpAfterSuccesses requires N consecutive echoes to come UP; DownAfter marks down after silence). Characterize it precisely — is there any DOWN-side hysteresis / ride-through today (hold-primary-through-a-short-gap), or does a single DownAfter of silence flip it DOWN immediately? Cite the Tick/RecordEcho logic.
4. FAILBACK dwell: is there a FailbackAfter / failback-dwell (the scheduler holding on the backup before returning to a recovered primary)? Cite it (internal/sched active_backup FailbackAfter). Does its non-configurability compound the thrash?
5. FEASIBILITY + CONSTRAINT: the P1 failover budget is 3s (thresholds.go); D15/D16 established DownAfter+interval must fit max(edge,conc)-detect+reroute within 3s. A ride-through / larger DownAfter must NOT regress that. Note where thresholds.go / the failover-budget composition lives so the fix can reason about the tradeoff (a config knob that lets an operator TRADE failover latency for stability on a metered link). Also: is DownAfter a SINGLE-SOURCE-OF-TRUTH consumed by both the daemon and test/e2e/thresholds.go (D16's fix) — so a config knob must feed both or the e2e budget analysis?

Return the evidence-json: {hypothesisId:"H-D86", evidence:[...], lean:"supports|refutes|inconclusive", notes:"...", probeRequest? }.

1	package telemetry
2	
3	import (
4		"time"
5	
6		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
7	)
8	
9	// Clock is the injectable time source the liveness machinery reads. Production
10	// code passes SystemClock; tests pass a hand-advanced fake so transitions are
11	// deterministic and instant.
12	type Clock interface {
13		Now() time.Time
14	}
15	
16	// SystemClock is the wall-clock Clock.
17	type SystemClock struct{}
18	
19	// Now returns the current wall-clock time.
20	func (SystemClock) Now() time.Time { return time.Now() }
21	
22	// PathState is the liveness verdict for one path. Path liveness is entirely this
23	// codec's concern: the inner WireGuard keepalive is per-peer, not per-path, so a
24	// dead uplink under a live peer is invisible to WireGuard and must be detected
25	// here.
26	type PathState uint8
27	
28	const (
29		// StateDown means no fresh probe echo has been observed within the detection
30		// threshold; the path is presumed unusable.
31		StateDown PathState = iota
32		// StateUp means the path has recently returned probe echoes.
33		StateUp
34	)
35	
36	// String renders the state as the lowercase token used in structured logs.
37	func (s PathState) String() string {
38		switch s {
39		case StateUp:
40			return "up"
41		case StateDown:
42			return "down"
43		default:
44			return "unknown"
45		}
46	}
47	
48	// LivenessConfig holds the up/down detection thresholds. DownAfter is the
49	// silence duration that marks an up path down; UpAfterSuccesses is the number of
50	// consecutive echoes required to bring a down path up. The asymmetry is the
51	// hysteresis: a single lost echo never flaps the path, and a single recovered
52	// echo never prematurely declares it healthy.
53	type LivenessConfig struct {
54		DownAfter        time.Duration
55		UpAfterSuccesses int
56	}
57	
58	// Default per-path liveness detection timing (T13/T37/T39). These are the SINGLE
59	// SOURCE OF TRUTH for probe cadence and up/down hysteresis. The daemon
60	// (internal/device) builds its ProberConfig from them and runs its probe loop at
61	// DefaultProbeInterval; the e2e acceptance table (test/e2e/thresholds.go) derives
62	// its PLiveness* budget from them. Neither can silently diverge from the timing the
63	// daemon actually runs, which is the D16 reconciliation: one place to retune.
64	//
65	//   - DefaultDownAfter is the silence that marks an UP path DOWN.
66	//   - DefaultProbeInterval is the probe emission AND liveness Tick cadence; detection
67	//     latency is therefore bounded by DownAfter + one interval.
68	//   - DefaultUpSuccesses is the consecutive echoes that bring a DOWN path UP.
69	//   - DefaultLossWindow (0) takes the estimator's default trailing window.
70	//
71	// DownAfter spans DefaultProbeInterval*6, so SIX consecutive lost echoes are needed
72	// before a false DOWN — the D13 jitter-safety margin. T39 tightened the pair from
73	// 1500ms/250ms to 1200ms/200ms to WIDEN the P1 3s-recovery margin while KEEPING
74	// that six-lost-echo false-down tolerance exactly (the ratio is unchanged). Detection
75	// latency drops from ~1.75s to ~1.4s worst case, and the receive-path liveness sweep
76	// (bind, D15) makes that detection robust to probe-loop-ticker starvation under load.
77	const (
78		DefaultProbeInterval = 200 * time.Millisecond
79		DefaultDownAfter     = 1200 * time.Millisecond
80		DefaultUpSuccesses   = 3
81		DefaultLossWindow    = 0
82	)
83	
84	// Liveness is the per-path up/down state machine. It is driven by two events:
85	// RecordEcho on every authenticated probe echo, and Tick on a periodic timer.
86	// It starts Down and requires UpAfterSuccesses echoes before declaring Up, so a
87	// path is never reported healthy before it has actually answered.
88	type Liveness struct {
89		cfg   LivenessConfig
90		clock Clock
91		log   log.Logger
92	
93		state      PathState
94		lastGood   time.Time
95		haveGood   bool
96		goodStreak int
97	}
98	
99	// NewLiveness builds a per-path liveness machine. The supplied logger is tagged
100	// with the path name so every transition record carries the per-path field.
101	func NewLiveness(pathName string, cfg LivenessConfig, clock Clock, logger log.Logger) *Liveness {
102		return &Liveness{
103			cfg:   cfg,
104			clock: clock,
105			log:   logger.Path(pathName),
106			state: StateDown,
107		}
108	}
109	
110	// RecordEcho registers a successful, authenticated probe echo as a liveness
111	// heartbeat. Only echoes that are CONSECUTIVE — no more than DownAfter apart —
112	// accumulate toward Up; an echo that arrives after a longer silence starts the
113	// streak over, so UpAfterSuccesses stray echoes spread across arbitrary silence
114	// never bring a Down path Up. Once UpAfterSuccesses consecutive heartbeats
115	// accumulate, a Down path transitions Up.
116	func (l *Liveness) RecordEcho() {
117		now := l.clock.Now()
118		if l.haveGood && now.Sub(l.lastGood) > l.cfg.DownAfter {
119			l.goodStreak = 0 // silence broke the run of consecutive echoes
120		}
121		l.lastGood = now
122		l.haveGood = true
123		l.goodStreak++
124		if l.state == StateDown && l.goodStreak >= l.cfg.UpAfterSuccesses {
125			l.transition(StateUp, now)
126		}
127	}
128	
129	// Tick re-evaluates liveness against the clock. An Up path with no heartbeat for
130	// longer than DownAfter transitions Down; a Down path that has gone silent past
131	// DownAfter has its partial up-streak reset, so recovery requires a fresh run of
132	// UpAfterSuccesses consecutive echoes rather than echoes accumulated before the
133	// silence. Call Tick at least as often as the probe interval so detection latency
134	// stays within DownAfter plus one interval.
135	func (l *Liveness) Tick() {
136		now := l.clock.Now()
137		if !l.haveGood {
138			return
139		}
140		stale := now.Sub(l.lastGood) > l.cfg.DownAfter
141		switch {
142		case l.state == StateUp && stale:
143			l.goodStreak = 0
144			l.transition(StateDown, now)
145		case l.state == StateDown && stale:
146			l.goodStreak = 0
147		}
148	}
149	
150	// State returns the current liveness verdict.
151	func (l *Liveness) State() PathState { return l.state }
152	
153	func (l *Liveness) transition(to PathState, now time.Time) {
154		from := l.state
155		l.state = to
156		l.log.Info("path liveness transition",
157			"from", from.String(),
158			"to", to.String(),
159			"silence_ms", now.Sub(l.lastGood).Milliseconds(),
160		)
161	}
162

1	// Package device brings a wanbond tunnel up from a validated configuration: it
2	// creates the TUN interface, drives the embedded amneziawg-go engine over the
3	// multipath Bind, and applies the WireGuard (and, when configured, amnezia
4	// obfuscation) parameters via the engine's UAPI. It owns ONLY the tunnel engine
5	// — interface addressing and routing are left to the operator (systemd/wg-quick
6	// style), so the daemon stays free of privileged shell-outs. The interface name
7	// is exposed via Tunnel.Name for that external configuration step.
8	package device
9	
10	import (
11		"context"
12		"crypto/rand"
13		"encoding/hex"
14		"errors"
15		"fmt"
16		"net/netip"
17		"reflect"
18		"strings"
19		"sync"
20		"sync/atomic"
21		"time"
22	
23		awgdevice "github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/device"
24		"github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/tun"
25	
26		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/adaptivefec"
27		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/bind"
28		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
29		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/dnsresolve"
30		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/fec"
31		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
32		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/metrics"
33		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/monitor"
34		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/sched"
35		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
36	)
37	
38	// metricsShutdownTimeout bounds the graceful shutdown of the /metrics endpoint on
39	// Close (and on a reload that rebinds it). The endpoint is loopback-only with no
40	// long-lived scrapes, so this is comfortably generous.
41	const metricsShutdownTimeout = 2 * time.Second
42	
43	// monitorShutdownTimeout bounds the graceful shutdown of the [monitor] endpoint on
44	// Close (and on a reload that reconciles it). It mirrors metricsShutdownTimeout;
45	// the monitor's /ws push handlers observe the server-context cancellation Close
46	// issues, so a stalled client cannot stretch teardown past this bound.
47	const monitorShutdownTimeout = 2 * time.Second
48	
49	// defaultTUNName is the requested interface name; the kernel honours it unless it
50	// collides (it never does across the edge and concentrator network namespaces).
51	const defaultTUNName = "wanbond0"
52	
53	// tunMTU is the tunnel (TUN) MTU for a config: the default path MTU minus the
54	// IP+UDP, outer DATA-frame, and WireGuard transport overheads, so a full-MTU inner
55	// packet never fragments on the wire (see internal/bind mtu.go and docs/p1-mtu.md).
56	// This is smaller than the plain-WireGuard 1420 because the bonding layer adds its
57	// own outer DATA frame per datagram; with FEC enabled it is a further
58	// bind.FECParityMTUPenalty smaller so a full-size PARITY frame also fits the path MTU
59	// (T24) rather than fragmenting.
60	func tunMTU(cfg *config.Config) int {
61		return bind.InnerMTU(bind.DefaultPathMTU, cfg.FEC.Enabled)
62	}
63	
64	// Tunnel is a running wanbond tunnel: the amneziawg engine, its TUN, and the
65	// multipath Bind. Close tears all three down.
66	type Tunnel struct {
67		dev  *awgdevice.Device
68		tun  tun.Device
69		name string
70		// routePrefixes is the default-route wiring installed into wanbond0 for a
71		// mode=default-route peer (I6, Q41): the wg-quick-style split of that peer's
72		// allowed_ips (the two /1s for a /0), installed after dev.Up and withdrawn on
73		// Close. Empty (nil) for a config with no default-route peer, so a plain tunnel
74		// programs — and tears down — no routes at all.
75		routePrefixes []netip.Prefix
76		// bind is the multipath Bind the engine drives; Reload calls its runtime
77		// AddPath/RemovePath to apply a config-reload path diff (T30).
78		bind *bind.Multipath
79		// cfg is the configuration the RUNNING tunnel reflects: the boot config, its path
80		// set updated on each successful Reload to the current membership (survivors keep
81		// their original parameters — a membership-only reload does not re-apply modified
82		// path parameters or non-path fields). Reload diffs the next desired config against
83		// it to warn about changes it cannot apply. Guarded by reloadMu.
84		cfg *config.Config
85		// reloadMu serializes Reload against itself (SIGHUP handlers), guarding cfg.
86		reloadMu sync.Mutex
87		// log is the device-component logger, retained for Reload diagnostics.
88		log log.Logger
89		// stopProbes halts the per-path probe cadence loop (T37). Close calls it before
90		// tearing the engine down so emission stops before the sockets close. It is
91		// idempotent and never nil (a no-op when the bind runs without probers).
92		stopProbes func()
93		// stopReconcile halts the background deferred-path reconcile loop (T55): it retries
94		// binding the paths a tolerant Open left DOWN as their addresses appear. Close calls
95		// it before tearing the engine down so no reconcile races the socket close. It is
96		// idempotent and never nil (a no-op when the bind runs without probers / never defers).
97		stopReconcile func()
98		// stopHubFailover halts the edge-side hub-failover monitor loop (T57): it watches the
99		// per-path liveness plane and, on hub loss (all paths to the active concentrator DOWN),
100		// advances to the next ordered peer endpoint, repoints the bond's remote, and initiates
101		// a fresh WG re-handshake. Close calls it before tearing the engine down. It is
102		// idempotent and never nil (a no-op on the concentrator, or an edge with a single
103		// concentrator endpoint, or a bind without the probe transport).
104		stopHubFailover func()
105		// stopResolution halts the edge-side DNS re-resolution loop (T74): it re-resolves each
106		// opted-in hostname peer-endpoint spec on the [dns] poll cadence and out-of-band on hub loss,
107		// feeding fresh records to the hub-failover controller. Close calls it between stopHubFailover
108		// and dev.Close. It is idempotent and never nil (a no-op on the concentrator, an all-literal
109		// edge, or when no hostname spec is configured — Q29 inertness).
110		stopResolution func()
111		// stopSession halts the WG-session monitor loop (I2): it polls the engine's last-handshake
112		// state at probe cadence and emits ONE INFO 'session established' record on each 0->1 edge.
113		// Close calls it before dev.Close so no IpcGet races the engine teardown. It is idempotent
114		// and never nil.
115		stopSession func()
116		// stopPeerTeardown halts the concentrator per-peer teardown monitor loop (D50/T126): in
117		// multi-peer mode it level-checks each configured non-primary peer's WG session and calls the
118		// bind's idempotent TearDownPeer on any peer whose session is gone, reclaiming its heavy
119		// receive state. Close calls it before dev.Close so no IpcGet races the engine teardown. It is
120		// idempotent and never nil (a no-op for a single-peer config, which monitors no non-primary peer).
121		stopPeerTeardown func()
122		// amnezia is the obfuscation profile this tunnel holds against the
123		// process-global amnezia guard (see amneziaGuard); Close releases it.
124		amnezia     config.Amnezia
125		releaseOnce sync.Once
126	
127		// metricsSrc is the live metrics.Source over the Bind; it is stable for the tunnel's
128		// life (the Bind pointer never changes), so a reload that rebinds the endpoint reuses
129		// the SAME Source — its derived-throughput last-sample state survives the rebind.
130		metricsSrc metrics.Source
131		// metricsSrv is the running /metrics endpoint, nil when [metrics].listen is empty.
132		// It is (re)assigned by applyMetricsLocked and read by Close; both hold reloadMu, so
133		// a SIGHUP-driven rebind never races shutdown. metricsListen mirrors the address it
134		// is bound to so a reload can detect a listen change without inspecting the server.
135		metricsSrv    *metrics.Server
136		metricsListen string
137	
138		// monitorSrc is the DEDICATED metrics.Source feeding the [monitor] endpoint. It is a
139		// SECOND Source over the SAME Bind — separate from metricsSrc — so the monitor's
140		// derived-throughput last-sample state is not shared with the Prometheus scraper: two
141		// independent scrape cadences reading one shared last-sample map would corrupt each
142		// other's rate derivation (T165 invariant). Like metricsSrc it is built unconditionally
143		// (cheap) so a reload that later turns [monitor].listen ON has a Source ready.
144		monitorSrc metrics.Source
145		// monitorSrv is the running [monitor] endpoint, nil when [monitor].listen is empty. It is
146		// (re)assigned by applyMonitorLocked and read by Close; both hold reloadMu, so a
147		// SIGHUP-driven reconcile never races shutdown. monitorListen/monitorToken mirror the
148		// address+token it is bound to so a reload can detect a listen OR token change (either
149		// forces a rebind) without inspecting the server.
150		monitorSrv    *monitor.Server
151		monitorListen string
152		monitorToken  string
153	}
154	
155	// SINGLE-ENGINE-PER-PROCESS INVARIANT (defect D2).
156	//
157	// amneziawg-go stores the amnezia magic-header message types in PACKAGE-GLOBAL
158	// variables — device.MessageInitiationType, MessageResponseType,
159	// MessageCookieReplyType, MessageTransportType. (*device.Device).IpcSet assigns
160	// them (in handlePostConfig) from a configured engine's profile, and
161	// (*device.Device).Close restores them to the WireGuard defaults (in
162	// resetProtocol) UNCONDITIONALLY — closing ANY engine, plain or configured,
163	// reverts the process-global message types to their defaults.
164	//
165	// Two consequences make a CONFIGURED (amnezia) engine require PROCESS
166	// EXCLUSIVITY:
167	//   - a second configured engine would overwrite the first engine's message-type
168	//     framing at IpcSet; and even with the SAME profile, closing the first engine
169	//     runs resetProtocol and reverts the globals to defaults under the second,
170	//     still-live engine, silently dropping its tunnel to plain-WireGuard framing.
171	//   - closing ANY other engine — a PLAIN (unconfigured) one included — runs
172	//     resetProtocol and resets the globals out from under a live configured engine.
173	//
174	// So the rule wanbond ASSERTS (rather than vendor-patching the fork) is:
175	//   - a configured engine may come up only when NO other engine is live, and
176	//   - no engine (plain or configured) may come up while a configured engine is live.
177	//
178	// PLAIN engines may coexist with each other: they never set the globals, and
179	// resetProtocol on their Close only restores the defaults they already use, so it
180	// is idempotent among them. wanbond runs exactly one tunnel per process, so the
181	// guard only ever trips on genuine misuse.
182	type amneziaGuard struct {
183		mu         sync.Mutex
184		plainLive  int  // number of live plain-WireGuard (unconfigured) engines
185		configLive bool // whether a configured (amnezia) engine is live (at most one)
186	}
187	
188	// globalAmneziaGuard enforces the single-amnezia-engine-per-process invariant for
189	// every Tunnel brought up in this process.
190	var globalAmneziaGuard amneziaGuard
191	
192	// acquire registers an about-to-start engine against the process-exclusivity rule
193	// (see amneziaGuard). A configured (amnezia) engine is admitted only when NO other
194	// engine is live. A plain engine is admitted only when no configured engine is
195	// live; plain engines may coexist with one another. The caller must release the
196	// SAME profile exactly once when the engine is torn down (plain engines included —
197	// release is no longer a no-op for them).
198	func (g *amneziaGuard) acquire(a config.Amnezia) error {
199		g.mu.Lock()
200		defer g.mu.Unlock()
201		if a.Configured() {
202			if g.configLive || g.plainLive > 0 {
203				return fmt.Errorf("device: refusing to start an amnezia-configured engine while another engine is live: " +
204					"amneziawg-go keeps the magic-header message types in process-global state and resets them to defaults on ANY engine's Close, " +
205					"so a configured engine requires process exclusivity — at most one, with no other engine alongside it (D2)")
206			}
207			g.configLive = true
208			return nil
209		}
210		if g.configLive {
211			return fmt.Errorf("device: refusing to start a second engine while an amnezia-configured engine is live: " +
212				"closing this engine would reset amneziawg-go's process-global message types to defaults under the live amnezia tunnel (D2)")
213		}
214		g.plainLive++
215		return nil
216	}
217	
218	// release drops an engine's hold on the guard. A configured engine clears the
219	// single configured slot; a plain engine decrements the live plain count.
220	func (g *amneziaGuard) release(a config.Amnezia) {
221		g.mu.Lock()
222		defer g.mu.Unlock()
223		if a.Configured() {
224			g.configLive = false
225			return
226		}
227		if g.plainLive > 0 {
228			g.plainLive--
229		}
230	}
231	
232	// Up creates the TUN, wires the multipath Bind into the amneziawg engine,
233	// applies the crypto configuration from cfg, and brings the device up. The same
234	// path drives both roles; the role only changes which UAPI fields cfg carries
235	// (the concentrator sets listen_port; the edge sets each peer's endpoint).
236	func Up(cfg *config.Config, lg log.Logger) (*Tunnel, error) {
237		clg := lg.Component("device")
238	
239		tunDev, err := tun.CreateTUN(defaultTUNName, tunMTU(cfg))
240		if err != nil {
241			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: create TUN: %w", err)
242		}
243		name, err := tunDev.Name()
244		if err != nil {
245			_ = tunDev.Close()
246			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: read TUN name: %w", err)
247		}
248		// Bring the interface administratively UP now (I1): amneziawg-go never does this
249		// itself, and a write to a DOWN TUN yields EIO — a silent-dead-tunnel footgun the
250		// operator previously had to work around with an out-of-band `ip link set up`.
251		// Addressing stays operator-owned; this sets ONLY the IFF_UP flag (see ifUp).
252		if err := ifUp(name); err != nil {
253			_ = tunDev.Close()
254			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: bring interface up: %w", err)
255		}
256		// Apply the TUN persistence policy (I7, Q38) BEFORE the amnezia single-engine
257		// guard in up(): with tun_persist=true the kernel keeps wanbond0 (and its
258		// operator-owned addresses/routes) across a daemon restart; with the default
259		// false it explicitly CLEARS the flag so the device disappears on Close exactly
260		// as before. This composes with the ifUp above and does not touch the reload or
261		// restart-on-failure paths — a reload never re-creates the TUN, and a persistent
262		// device is simply re-adopted by name on the next Up (see setTUNPersist).
263		if err := setTUNPersist(tunDev, cfg.TUNPersist); err != nil {
264			_ = tunDev.Close()
265			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: set TUN persistence: %w", err)
266		}
267		clg.Info("interface up", "interface", name, "tun_persist", cfg.TUNPersist)
268		// The DNS re-resolution transport is the one the (validated) [dns] block selects. It is passed
269		// as a factory (not eagerly constructed) so up() builds it AT MOST ONCE and ONLY when some peer
270		// carries a hostname endpoint spec — a config with zero hostname specs never constructs a
271		// resolver (Q29 inertness). The injected TUN and factory are also the seams device tests drive.
272		return up(cfg, clg, tunDev, name, cfg.DNS.NewResolver)
273	}
274	
275	// resolverFactory builds the DNS resolver used for hostname endpoint re-resolution. It is injected
276	// so a device test can supply a fake; production passes cfg.DNS.NewResolver. up() invokes it AT
277	// MOST ONCE, and ONLY when some peer carries a hostname endpoint spec.
278	type resolverFactory func() (dnsresolve.Resolver, error)
279	
280	// up drives the tunnel bring-up over an already-created TUN and an injected resolver factory: it
281	// wires the multipath Bind into the amneziawg engine, performs the bounded initial hostname
282	// resolve (Q30), applies the crypto/endpoint UAPI config built ONLY from resolved entries, brings
283	// the device up, and starts the probe / reconcile / hub-failover / re-resolution loops. Splitting
284	// it out of Up gives device tests a seam to inject a channel TUN and a fake resolver without the
285	// privileged tun.CreateTUN. The same path drives both roles; the role only changes which UAPI
286	// fields cfg carries (the concentrator sets listen_port; the edge sets each peer's endpoint).
287	func up(cfg *config.Config, clg log.Logger, tunDev tun.Device, name string, newResolver resolverFactory) (*Tunnel, error) {
288		// Wrap the TUN so a write that fails with EIO gets an actionable, rate-limited diagnostic
289		// (naming the interface's link state/MTU and the remedy) alongside the raw errno, instead
290		// of surfacing only as the engine's generic "input/output error" (D39, I3). Every other
291		// Device method is unaffected.
292		tunDev = newDiagnosingTUN(tunDev, name, clg)
293	
294		// Claim the single-amnezia-engine-per-process invariant (D2) BEFORE IpcSet
295		// assigns amneziawg-go's process-global message-type state. On any failure
296		// below, ok stays false and the deferred release returns the hold; the
297		// successful path transfers the hold to the returned Tunnel.
298		if err := globalAmneziaGuard.acquire(cfg.Amnezia); err != nil {
299			_ = tunDev.Close()
300			return nil, err
301		}
302		ok := false
303		defer func() {
304			if !ok {
305				globalAmneziaGuard.release(cfg.Amnezia)
306			}
307		}()
308	
309		// One random per-boot probe session id, shared by every path AND every configured peer (it
310		// identifies THIS boot, not a path or peer): a peer restart presents a new id that resets
311		// the surviving responder's anti-replay high-water so liveness recovers (T38, D12), and a
312		// runtime-added path (T30) reuses it so its probes join this boot's stream.
313		sessionID, err := telemetry.NewSessionID(rand.Reader)
314		if err != nil {
315			_ = tunDev.Close()
316			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: new probe session id: %w", err)
317		}
318		// PeerIdentities is the SINGLE place the single-peer/multi-peer effective-PSK decision is
319		// made (G4): for a single-peer config every identity's PSK is the top-level psk; for a
320		// multi-peer concentrator each is that peer's own psk. Its order matches cfg.WireGuard.Peers
321		// (and thus uapiConfig's peer render), so the engine peer set and the Bind peer set agree.
322		ids := cfg.PeerIdentities()
323		// The primary peer (peers[0]) drives on-wire liveness through the SAME prober values that
324		// back its scheduler's PathHealth (T37). It is keyed on the FIRST identity's effective psk:
325		// the top-level psk for a single-peer config (byte-identical to pre-G4), or peer 0's own psk
326		// for a multi-peer concentrator.
327		scheduler, probers, newProber, err := buildScheduler(cfg, ids[0].PSK, sessionID, clg)
328		if err != nil {
329			_ = tunDev.Close()
330			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: build scheduler: %w", err)
331		}
332		mpBind, err := bind.NewMultipath(cfg.Paths, ids[0].PSK, scheduler, probers, newProber, fecConfig(cfg.FEC), adaptiveFECConfig(cfg.FEC), cfg.Amnezia, clg)
333		if err != nil {
334			_ = tunDev.Close()
335			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: build multipath bind: %w", err)
336		}
337		// A multi-peer concentrator names its primary too (D58): NewMultipath always mints the
338		// primary as peers[0] with name="" (a single-peer edge/hub needs no name — there is only
339		// one peer to key, and this keeps that exposition byte-identical to pre-T94). Once a
340		// SECOND identity is configured, every bound peer — including the primary — must carry its
341		// configured name so /metrics attributes series to the right edge instead of leaking the
342		// primary as peer="". This runs BEFORE the AddConcentratorPeer loop below so that loop's
343		// name-collision checks (name == m.name, the peersByName duplicate check) compare against
344		// the primary's FINAL name.
345		if len(ids) > 1 {
346			if err := mpBind.SetPrimaryPeerName(ids[0].Name); err != nil {
347				_ = tunDev.Close()
348				return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: set primary peer name %q: %w", ids[0].Name, err)
349			}
350		}
351		// Concentrator per-peer wiring (G4/T93): register each ADDITIONAL configured peer with its
352		// OWN prober set, scheduler, and prober factory (all keyed on that peer's effective psk),
353		// BEFORE dev.Up drives the bind's Open — which builds each registered peer's per-(peer,path)
354		// view of every bound socket, reconciles its scheduler, and reports its stable virtual
355		// endpoint to the engine (A1). A single-peer config has exactly one identity, so this loop
356		// is empty and the wiring stays byte-identical to the pre-G4 single-peer path.
357		for _, id := range ids[1:] {
358			psched, pprobers, pfactory, perr := buildScheduler(cfg, id.PSK, sessionID, clg)
359			if perr != nil {
360				_ = tunDev.Close()
361				return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: build scheduler for peer %q: %w", id.Name, perr)
362			}
363			if perr := mpBind.AddConcentratorPeer(id.Name, id.PSK, psched, pprobers, pfactory); perr != nil {
364				_ = tunDev.Close()
365				return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: wire concentrator peer %q: %w", id.Name, perr)
366			}
367		}
368		dev := awgdevice.NewDevice(tunDev, mpBind, engineLogger(clg, cfg.Log.Level, mpBind.EverHadLivePath))
369	
370		// Drive a forced WG handshake initiation off the bind's first-path-up latch (T117/D37/T120):
371		// registered HERE, before StartProbeLoop below can flip any path Up — see
372		// startFirstPathUpHandshake's doc comment for why the ordering matters and why it is inert for
373		// the concentrator role (peers[0] is the edge's sole peer per config validation; unused, and
374		// harmless, when cfg.Role is concentrator).
375		startFirstPathUpHandshake(cfg, mpBind, deviceRehandshake(dev, cfg.WireGuard.Peers[0].PublicKey))
376	
377		// Bounded initial hostname resolution (Q30): construct the resolver ONCE (only when some peer
378		// carries a hostname spec — Q29 inertness), resolve each hostname spec under a short timeout,
379		// and seed its expansion. The per-peer boot endpoint — the flattened head of the seeded specs —
380		// is what the UAPI render installs on the engine peer; a hostname that does not resolve in the
381		// boot window (or a resolver that fails to construct) leaves its spec EMPTY and the peer boots
382		// WITHOUT a peer endpoint (tolerant boot). The re-resolution loop then completes the wiring on
383		// first success via the FIRST-RESOLVE INSTALL PATH (R70). Literal specs are seeded verbatim, so
384		// an all-literal peer boots byte-for-byte as before.
385		boot := resolveBootEndpoints(cfg, newResolver, clg)
386	
387		uapi, err := uapiConfig(cfg, boot.endpoints)
388		if err != nil {
389			dev.Close()
390			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: build UAPI config: %w", err)
391		}
392		if err := dev.IpcSet(uapi); err != nil {
393			dev.Close()
394			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: apply UAPI config: %w", err)
395		}
396		if err := dev.Up(); err != nil {
397			dev.Close()
398			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: bring up: %w", err)
399		}
400	
401		// Default-route wiring (I6, Q41): with a mode=default-route peer, install the
402		// wg-quick-style split default route (the two /1s of that peer's allowed_ips) INTO
403		// wanbond0 now that the interface is up and the engine is running, so an arbitrary
404		// destination egresses through the tunnel. This is the daemon's FIRST route
405		// programming — confined to the mode being explicitly enabled: a config with no
406		// default-route peer computes an empty set, opens no netlink socket, and installs
407		// nothing, so default behaviour is byte-for-byte unchanged. STRICT Q41 boundary —
408		// routes ONLY (no policy routing, no SNAT, no concentrator forwarding). Fail fast:
409		// a route error aborts bring-up. Before returning we best-effort withdraw whatever
410		// partial set installRoutes landed: dev.Close tears down the engine/TUN, but under
411		// tun_persist=true the interface (and its routes) SURVIVES Close, and up() aborts
412		// before the Tunnel exists so Close/removeRoutes never runs — the already-installed
413		// prefixes would otherwise leak on the surviving interface. removeRoutes errors are
414		// ignored (the install error is the one that matters; a leftover is overwritten by
415		// the next successful start's NLM_F_REPLACE re-install anyway). Removed on Close.
416		routePrefixes := defaultRoutePrefixes(cfg)
417		if len(routePrefixes) > 0 {
418			if err := installRoutes(name, routePrefixes); err != nil {
419				_ = removeRoutes(name, routePrefixes)
420				dev.Close()
421				return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: install default-route wiring: %w", err)
422			}
423			clg.Info("default-route wiring installed", "interface", name, "routes", len(routePrefixes))
424		}
425	
426		// The engine has opened the bind (dev.Up → BindUpdate → Open), so the per-path
427		// sockets exist: start the probe cadence now. Close stops it before dev.Close.
428		// The interval is the SINGLE-SOURCE-OF-TRUTH telemetry default, which also arms
429		// the bind's receive-path liveness sweep throttle (D15).
430		stopProbes := mpBind.StartProbeLoop(telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval)
431		// Start the background deferred-path reconciler alongside probing (T55): a path the
432		// tolerant Open left DOWN (its source_addr not yet assignable — a 5G modem without a
433		// lease at boot) is retried and promoted to a live path as its address appears,
434		// WITHOUT a tunnel restart. Close stops it before dev.Close, like the probe loop.
435		stopReconcile := mpBind.StartReconcileLoop(bind.DefaultReconcileInterval)
436		// Start the edge-side hub-failover monitor (T57) AND the DNS re-resolution loop (T74): on hub
437		// loss (every path to the active concentrator DOWN) failover advances to the next ordered peer
438		// endpoint, repoints the bond's remote, and re-handshakes; re-resolution re-resolves opted-in
439		// hostname specs and installs/repoints on change. Both a no-op on the concentrator, a
440		// single-IP-literal edge, or a bind without the probe transport. Started AFTER dev.Up so the
441		// engine peer the re-handshake / endpoint-install look up exists (IpcSet added it above). Close
442		// stops both before dev.Close, like the probe/reconcile loops.
443		stopHubFailover, stopResolution := startFailoverAndResolution(cfg, mpBind, probers, dev, boot, clg)
444	
445		// The session monitor reads the engine's peer last-handshake state (I2). It backs BOTH
446		// the /metrics session snapshot (read at scrape time) and the 0->1 'session established'
447		// edge log (driven by its own poll loop). One instance is shared: it is stateless, so the
448		// concurrent scrape goroutine and the poll loop reading the same engine is safe.
449		sessMon := newSessionMonitor(dev, telemetry.SystemClock{})
450		// Start the WG-session monitor poll loop AFTER dev.Up so the engine peer it reads exists.
451		// Close stops it before dev.Close, like the probe/reconcile loops.
452		stopSession := startSessionMonitor(sessMon, sessionPollInterval, clg)
453		// Start the concentrator per-peer teardown monitor (D50/T126): in MULTI-PEER mode it
454		// level-checks each configured non-primary peer's WG session every poll and calls the bind's
455		// idempotent TearDownPeer on any peer whose session is gone (no handshake, or aged past
456		// RejectAfterTime), reclaiming its resequencer ring, FEC buffers, and demux cap slots — the
457		// leak the edge-triggered global monitor cannot catch (a valid-psk peer that binds via PROBE
458		// but never completes a handshake produces no 1->0 edge). concentratorMonitoredPeers returns
459		// an EMPTY peer set for a single-peer config, so the loop is a no-op there and the single-peer
460		// monitor path is byte-identical to pre-T126. Started after dev.Up (the engine peers it reads
461		// exist); Close stops it before dev.Close.
462		teardownMon := newPeerTeardownMonitor(dev, mpBind, concentratorMonitoredPeers(cfg, ids), telemetry.SystemClock{})
463		stopPeerTeardown := startPeerTeardownMonitor(teardownMon, sessionPollInterval, clg)
464	
465		t := &Tunnel{
466			dev: dev, tun: tunDev, name: name, bind: mpBind, cfg: cfg, log: clg,
467			routePrefixes: routePrefixes,
468			stopProbes:    stopProbes, stopReconcile: stopReconcile,
469			stopHubFailover: stopHubFailover, stopResolution: stopResolution,
470			stopSession: stopSession, stopPeerTeardown: stopPeerTeardown, amnezia: cfg.Amnezia,
471			// The Source reads live per-path counters/telemetry from the Bind and derives
472			// throughput from the byte-counter delta between scrapes (see metricsSource). The
473			// WG-session snapshot is read from the engine via sessMon. It is built unconditionally
474			// (cheap) so a reload that later turns [metrics].listen ON has a Source ready; the
475			// endpoint itself is started only when a listen is configured.
476			metricsSrc: newMetricsSource(mpBind, sessMon, telemetry.SystemClock{}),
477			// A SECOND, DEDICATED Source for the [monitor] endpoint over the SAME Bind/session
478			// seam — NOT metricsSrc. The two endpoints scrape on independent cadences and each
479			// Source derives throughput from its OWN last-sample state, so sharing one Source
480			// between them would let each scrape reset the other's rate baseline (T165). Built
481			// unconditionally so a reload can turn [monitor].listen ON with a Source ready.
482			monitorSrc: newMetricsSource(mpBind, sessMon, telemetry.SystemClock{}),
483		}
484	
485		// Stand up the /metrics endpoint when configured. A non-loopback listen is refused
486		// by metrics.NewServer (fail fast) — surface it as an Up failure rather than booting
487		// a tunnel that silently exposes per-path operational data off-host.
488		t.reloadMu.Lock()
489		err = t.applyMetricsLocked(cfg.Metrics.Listen)
490		t.reloadMu.Unlock()
491		if err != nil {
492			// The tunnel is fully constructed and holds the amnezia guard, so transfer that
493			// ownership to it (ok=true) BEFORE tearing it down: t.Close releases the guard via
494			// releaseOnce, and suppressing the !ok defer avoids a double release. t.Close also
495			// stops the probe loop and closes the engine/TUN.
496			ok = true
497			t.Close()
498			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: start metrics endpoint: %w", err)
499		}
500	
501		// Stand up the [monitor] endpoint when configured, mirroring the /metrics endpoint
502		// above but fed by the DEDICATED t.monitorSrc. A non-loopback listen without a token
503		// is refused by monitor.NewServer (fail fast) — surface it as an Up failure rather than
504		// booting a tunnel that silently exposes operational data off-host. Because role is a
505		// config-only field and BOTH RoleEdge and RoleConcentrator flow through up(), this one
506		// wiring gives edge+concentrator monitor parity.
507		t.reloadMu.Lock()
508		err = t.applyMonitorLocked(cfg.Monitor.Listen, cfg.Monitor.Token)
509		t.reloadMu.Unlock()
510		if err != nil {
511			ok = true
512			t.Close()
513			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: start monitor endpoint: %w", err)
514		}
515	
516		ok = true
517		clg.Info("tunnel up", "interface", name, "role", string(cfg.Role))
518		return t, nil
519	}

521	// concentratorMonitoredPeers returns the set of NON-PRIMARY configured peers the per-peer
522	// teardown monitor (D50/T126) level-checks: every peer after peers[0], paired with the stable
523	// name TearDownPeer is keyed on (from PeerIdentities, so the single-peer/multi-peer name
524	// derivation is shared) and the lowercase-hex public key the engine's UAPI dump identifies it
525	// by (hex.EncodeToString(pub[:]), the same form uapiConfig renders). ids MUST be
526	// cfg.PeerIdentities() — its order matches cfg.WireGuard.Peers, so index i pairs peer i's
527	// public key with identity i's name. A single-peer config (edge/hub, or a one-peer
528	// concentrator) yields an EMPTY set, so the teardown loop is inert and the pre-T126 single-peer
529	// monitor path is unchanged. The primary (peers[0]) is excluded: TearDownPeer refuses it, and
530	// its lifecycle is Open/Close, not session teardown.
531	func concentratorMonitoredPeers(cfg *config.Config, ids []config.PeerIdentity) []monitoredPeer {
532		peers := cfg.WireGuard.Peers
533		if len(peers) <= 1 {
534			return nil
535		}
536		mon := make([]monitoredPeer, 0, len(peers)-1)
537		for i := 1; i < len(peers); i++ {
538			pub := peers[i].PublicKey.Bytes()
539			mon = append(mon, monitoredPeer{name: ids[i].Name, publicKey: hex.EncodeToString(pub[:])})
540		}
541		return mon
542	}

544	// applyMetricsLocked reconciles the running /metrics endpoint to listen: it starts the
545	// endpoint when one is desired and none runs, stops it when listen is empty, and rebinds
546	// (stop old, start new) when the address changed. It is idempotent for an unchanged
547	// address. The Source is reused across a rebind so its derived-throughput state is not
548	// reset. The caller MUST hold reloadMu (Up and Reload both do), which also serializes it
549	// against Close reading metricsSrv. On a NewServer/refuse error the previous server is
550	// left running untouched, so a bad reload never drops a working endpoint.
551	func (t *Tunnel) applyMetricsLocked(listen string) error {
552		if listen == t.metricsListen {
553			return nil
554		}
555		if listen == "" {
556			t.stopMetricsLocked()
557			return nil
558		}
559		srv, err := metrics.NewServer(listen, t.metricsSrc, t.cfg.WeightedCapacitySane, t.log)
560		if err != nil {
561			return err
562		}
563		t.stopMetricsLocked()
564		srv.Start()
565		t.metricsSrv = srv
566		t.metricsListen = listen
567		return nil
568	}
569	
570	// stopMetricsLocked gracefully shuts the running endpoint down (if any) and clears the
571	// bookkeeping. Caller holds reloadMu.
572	func (t *Tunnel) stopMetricsLocked() {
573		if t.metricsSrv == nil {
574			return
575		}
576		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), metricsShutdownTimeout)
577		defer cancel()
578		if err := t.metricsSrv.Close(ctx); err != nil {
579			t.log.Warn("metrics endpoint shutdown error", "error", err.Error())
580		}
581		t.metricsSrv = nil
582		t.metricsListen = ""
583	}
584	
585	// applyMonitorLocked reconciles the running [monitor] endpoint to (listen, token): it
586	// starts the endpoint when one is desired and none runs, stops it when listen is empty,
587	// and rebinds when EITHER the address OR the token changed — a token change alters the
588	// auth layer, so it must rebind even at an unchanged address. It is idempotent for an
589	// unchanged (listen, token) pair. The dedicated t.monitorSrc is reused across a rebind so
590	// its derived-throughput state is not reset. The caller MUST hold reloadMu (Up and Reload
591	// both do), which also serializes it against Close reading monitorSrv. The rebind ORDER is
592	// address-dependent (see the body): a same-address (token-only) rebind stops the old
593	// server before binding the new to avoid EADDRINUSE at a fixed port; an address-changing
594	// rebind binds the new server first and only stops the old on success, so a bad
595	// address-changing reload never drops a working endpoint. Mirrors applyMetricsLocked.
596	func (t *Tunnel) applyMonitorLocked(listen, token string) error {
597		if listen == t.monitorListen && token == t.monitorToken {
598			return nil
599		}
600		if listen == "" {
601			t.stopMonitorLocked()
602			return nil
603		}
604		// Rebind ordering depends on whether the LISTEN ADDRESS changes:
605		//   - SAME address (e.g. a token-only rotation): the old listener still
606		//     holds the port, so the new bind would collide (EADDRINUSE). Stop the
607		//     old server FIRST, then bind the new. A NewServer failure here leaves
608		//     the endpoint DOWN, but the address is unchanged and a bad token was
609		//     already rejected by config.validate at load, so this is unexpected.
610		//   - DIFFERENT address: the new bind is on a different port, so bind the
611		//     new server FIRST and only stop the old on success — preserving the
612		//     "a bad reload never drops a working endpoint" property.
613		sameAddr := listen == t.monitorListen
614		if sameAddr {
615			t.stopMonitorLocked()
616		}
617		srv, err := monitor.NewServer(listen, token, t.monitorSrc, t.log)
618		if err != nil {
619			return err
620		}
621		if !sameAddr {
622			t.stopMonitorLocked()
623		}
624		srv.Start()
625		t.monitorSrv = srv
626		t.monitorListen = listen
627		t.monitorToken = token
628		return nil
629	}
630	
631	// stopMonitorLocked gracefully shuts the running [monitor] endpoint down (if any) and
632	// clears the bookkeeping. Caller holds reloadMu. Mirrors stopMetricsLocked.
633	func (t *Tunnel) stopMonitorLocked() {
634		if t.monitorSrv == nil {
635			return
636		}
637		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), monitorShutdownTimeout)
638		defer cancel()
639		if err := t.monitorSrv.Close(ctx); err != nil {
640			t.log.Warn("monitor endpoint shutdown error", "error", err.Error())
641		}
642		t.monitorSrv = nil
643		t.monitorListen = ""
644		t.monitorToken = ""
645	}

647	// Reload applies a reloaded configuration to the RUNNING tunnel by diffing its
648	// path set against the live one and adding/removing paths at runtime (T30), WITHOUT
649	// tearing the tunnel down: the WG session, the surviving paths, and in-flight
650	// resequencing are all preserved. cfg is assumed already validated (config.Load
651	// fails fast on a bad file, so a bad reload never reaches here and never disturbs
652	// the running tunnel). Only the path set is diffed — the crypto/amnezia/role fields
653	// are fixed for the life of the process and a reload does not re-key the engine.
654	//
655	// New paths are added BEFORE absent ones are removed, so a failed addition aborts
656	// with every existing path still in service. Diffing is by name: a path present in
657	// both sets is left untouched (its source/dest parameters are not re-read).
658	//
659	// A membership-only reload CANNOT apply every kind of change: a same-name path whose
660	// source/dest changed, a path REORDER (index 0 is the preferred primary, so a reorder
661	// is a priority change), and every non-path field (psk, wireguard, amnezia, role, log,
662	// metrics) are all out of scope for T30. Rather than silently diverge from the file,
663	// Reload logs an EXPLICIT WARNING per ignored change (reloadWarnings) so the operator
664	// knows exactly what was dropped; only the path-membership add/remove is applied.
665	func (t *Tunnel) Reload(cfg *config.Config) error {
666		t.reloadMu.Lock()
667		defer t.reloadMu.Unlock()
668	
669		for _, w := range reloadWarnings(t.cfg, cfg) {
670			t.log.Warn("reload: ignored config change (path-membership reload only)", "change", w)
671		}
672	
673		// Rebind the /metrics endpoint if its listen address changed (T23). This is one of
674		// the few non-path fields a reload DOES apply: the endpoint is external to the WG
675		// session and the Bind, so restarting it disturbs neither. applyMetricsLocked reuses
676		// the tunnel's stable Source (its derived-throughput state survives), and on a refuse
677		// (e.g. a newly non-loopback address) leaves the previous endpoint running and fails
678		// the reload — the running tunnel is never disturbed. Applied BEFORE the path diff so
679		// a metrics-refuse aborts before any membership change.
680		if cfg.Metrics.Listen != t.metricsListen {
681			if err := t.applyMetricsLocked(cfg.Metrics.Listen); err != nil {
682				return fmt.Errorf("device: reload metrics endpoint: %w", err)
683			}
684			t.log.Info("reload: metrics endpoint rebound", "listen", cfg.Metrics.Listen)
685		}
686	
687		// Reconcile the [monitor] endpoint (T169) — the SECOND non-path field a reload applies,
688		// mirroring /metrics. A listen OR token change reconciles the endpoint (start/stop/rebind)
689		// via the dedicated t.monitorSrc without disturbing the WG session or the Bind. On a refuse
690		// (e.g. a newly non-loopback address without a token) the previous endpoint is left running
691		// and the reload fails. Applied BEFORE the path diff so a monitor-refuse aborts before any
692		// membership change.
693		if cfg.Monitor.Listen != t.monitorListen || cfg.Monitor.Token != t.monitorToken {
694			if err := t.applyMonitorLocked(cfg.Monitor.Listen, cfg.Monitor.Token); err != nil {
695				return fmt.Errorf("device: reload monitor endpoint: %w", err)
696			}
697			t.log.Info("reload: monitor endpoint reconciled", "listen", cfg.Monitor.Listen)
698		}
699	
700		add, remove := diffPaths(t.bind.PathNames(), cfg.Paths)
701		for _, def := range add {
702			if err := t.bind.AddPath(def); err != nil {
703				return fmt.Errorf("device: reload add path %q: %w", def.Name, err)
704			}
705			t.log.Info("reload: path added", "path", def.Name)
706		}
707		for _, name := range remove {
708			if err := t.bind.RemovePath(name); err != nil {
709				return fmt.Errorf("device: reload remove path %q: %w", name, err)
710			}
711			t.log.Info("reload: path removed", "path", name)
712		}
713		// D74: recompute the config-derived weighted-capacity-sanity verdict from the reloaded
714		// config and re-set the retained gauge on the running /metrics endpoint, so a reload
715		// that changed the path set (add/remove) updates wanbond_weighted_capacity_sane instead
716		// of leaving it frozen at the boot value. WARN when the verdict diverges from the
717		// running one so the operator sees the sanity flip. Skipped when no endpoint runs, or
718		// under the active-backup policy (a nil verdict — the series is absent and the scheduler
719		// policy is fixed for the process, so a reload never introduces it).
720		if t.metricsSrv != nil && cfg.WeightedCapacitySane != nil {
721			newSane := *cfg.WeightedCapacitySane
722			if t.cfg.WeightedCapacitySane == nil || *t.cfg.WeightedCapacitySane != newSane {
723				t.log.Warn("reload: weighted-capacity-sanity verdict changed", "sane", newSane)
724			}
725			t.metricsSrv.SetWeightedCapacitySane(newSane)
726		}
727	
728		// Advance the running config to the membership now in service. Survivors keep their
729		// ORIGINAL parameters and all non-path fields stay as booted (the ignored changes
730		// were not applied), so a subsequent identical reload re-warns about a still-diverged
731		// file rather than silently accepting it. Metrics.Listen is carried to the applied
732		// value so a subsequent reload diffs against the endpoint actually running.
733		t.cfg = runningConfig(t.cfg, add, remove)
734		t.cfg.Metrics.Listen = t.metricsListen
735		// Carry the applied [monitor] state so a subsequent reload diffs against the endpoint
736		// actually running (mirroring Metrics.Listen above).
737		t.cfg.Monitor.Listen = t.monitorListen
738		t.cfg.Monitor.Token = t.monitorToken
739		// Carry the recomputed weighted-capacity verdict so a subsequent reload's divergence
740		// check compares against the value the gauge now holds (D74).
741		t.cfg.WeightedCapacitySane = cfg.WeightedCapacitySane
742		return nil
743	}
744	
745	// reloadWarnings compares the desired config against the currently-running one and
746	// returns one human-readable warning per change a path-membership-only reload (T30)
747	// cannot apply: a modified same-name path (source/dest), a path reorder, and any
748	// non-path field. Membership add/remove is NOT reported here — it is applied. Full
749	// modify-support is out of scope; SILENCE is not — the operator must be told what was
750	// ignored. It is a pure function so the warning set is unit-testable.
751	func reloadWarnings(live, desired *config.Config) []string {
752		var w []string
753	
754		if live.Role != desired.Role {
755			w = append(w, fmt.Sprintf("role %q -> %q", live.Role, desired.Role))
756		}
757		if !reflect.DeepEqual(live.PSK, desired.PSK) {
758			w = append(w, "psk changed")
759		}
760		if !reflect.DeepEqual(live.WireGuard, desired.WireGuard) {
761			w = append(w, "wireguard section changed")
762		}
763		if !reflect.DeepEqual(live.Amnezia, desired.Amnezia) {
764			w = append(w, "amnezia section changed")
765		}
766		if !reflect.DeepEqual(live.Log, desired.Log) {
767			w = append(w, "log section changed")
768		}
769		// tun_persist is applied only once, at device.Up (TUNSETPERSIST); a reload never
770		// re-issues the ioctl, so a SIGHUP that flips it must NOT be silently accepted —
771		// otherwise an operator who sets tun_persist=true and reloads believes persistence
772		// is armed while the next stop still destroys wanbond0 and drops its addressing.
773		if live.TUNPersist != desired.TUNPersist {
774			w = append(w, fmt.Sprintf("tun_persist %v -> %v — the running interface keeps its original persistence; ignored until restart", live.TUNPersist, desired.TUNPersist))
775		}
776		// NOTE: neither a Metrics nor a Monitor change is warned here — unlike the other
777		// non-path fields, the reload APPLIES both by rebinding their endpoints (see Reload,
778		// T169). Warning about a change that is honoured would misinform the operator.
779		if !reflect.DeepEqual(live.Scheduler, desired.Scheduler) {
780			w = append(w, "scheduler section changed — the running scheduler keeps its original policy/parameters until restart")
781		}
782		if !reflect.DeepEqual(live.FEC, desired.FEC) {
783			w = append(w, "fec section changed — the running FEC parameters are unchanged until restart")
784		}
785		if !reflect.DeepEqual(live.DNS, desired.DNS) {
786			w = append(w, "dns section changed — the running resolver configuration is unchanged until restart")
787		}
788		// The top-level Bind default (I5, Q42) is resolved by normalize() into every path
789		// that omits its own `bind`, so a change here has already propagated into
790		// Path.Bind by the time reloadWarnings runs — but the RUNNING sockets keep the
791		// binding they were opened with, so it still needs its own actionable warning
792		// (kept separate from the generic catch-all below to avoid double-warning).
793		if live.Bind != desired.Bind {
794			w = append(w, "default bind mode changed — running sockets keep their original binding")
795		}
796	
797		// Same-name paths whose parameters changed: diffPaths matches by name only, so a
798		// modified source/dest on an existing path is otherwise silently dropped.
799		liveByName := make(map[string]config.Path, len(live.Paths))
800		for _, p := range live.Paths {
801			liveByName[p.Name] = p
802		}
803		for _, d := range desired.Paths {
804			l, ok := liveByName[d.Name]
805			if !ok {
806				continue // a genuinely new path is added, not ignored
807			}
808			if l.SourceAddr != d.SourceAddr || l.DestAddr != d.DestAddr {
809				w = append(w, fmt.Sprintf("path %q source/dest changed — the running path keeps its original binding", d.Name))
810			}
811			if l.Bind != d.Bind {
812				w = append(w, fmt.Sprintf("path %q bind mode changed — the running socket keeps its original binding", d.Name))
813			}
814			// D70: a same-name path's declared link capacity (link_bandwidth/link_rtt) is NOT
815			// applied by a membership reload — the running path keeps its original pacing/weight
816			// — and the D52 catch-all zeroes Paths before its DeepEqual, so a change here is
817			// otherwise silent. Warn so the operator knows the new declaration is deferred.
818			if l.LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec != d.LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec || l.LinkRTT != d.LinkRTT {
819				w = append(w, fmt.Sprintf("path %q link_bandwidth/link_rtt changed — the running path keeps its original capacity declaration; restart required", d.Name))
820			}
821		}
822	
823		if reordered(live.Paths, desired.Paths) {
824			w = append(w, "path priority order changed (reorder) — the running order is unchanged")
825		}
826	
827		// D52 option B — future-proof catch-all: copy live/desired with every
828		// INDIVIDUALLY handled field zeroed (including Paths and Metrics — Metrics IS
829		// applied by Reload, see the NOTE above, so it must not warn here either) and
830		// DeepEqual the copies. A Config field added later that nobody has taught this
831		// function to warn about falls through to this generic warning instead of being
832		// silently accepted, so the "SILENCE is not acceptable" invariant (D52) cannot
833		// regress by omission.
834		lc, dc := *live, *desired
835		lc.Role, dc.Role = "", ""
836		lc.PSK, dc.PSK = config.Key{}, config.Key{}
837		lc.WireGuard, dc.WireGuard = config.WireGuard{}, config.WireGuard{}
838		lc.Amnezia, dc.Amnezia = config.Amnezia{}, config.Amnezia{}
839		lc.Log, dc.Log = config.Log{}, config.Log{}
840		lc.TUNPersist, dc.TUNPersist = false, false
841		lc.Scheduler, dc.Scheduler = config.SchedulerConfig{}, config.SchedulerConfig{}
842		lc.FEC, dc.FEC = config.FEC{}, config.FEC{}
843		lc.DNS, dc.DNS = config.DNS{}, config.DNS{}
844		lc.Bind, dc.Bind = "", ""
845		lc.Paths, dc.Paths = nil, nil
846		lc.Metrics, dc.Metrics = config.Metrics{}, config.Metrics{}
847		lc.Monitor, dc.Monitor = config.Monitor{}, config.Monitor{}
848		// WeightedCapacitySane (T144) is a value COMPUTED from Scheduler+Paths, never an
849		// independent operator knob (toml:"-") — a change to it is always a symptom of a
850		// Scheduler or Paths change, both already compared above (or, for a same-name
851		// path's link_bandwidth specifically, a pre-existing gap outside T144's scope).
852		// Comparing it directly here would be redundant at best and could double-warn.
853		lc.WeightedCapacitySane, dc.WeightedCapacitySane = nil, nil
854		if !reflect.DeepEqual(lc, dc) {
855			w = append(w, "other config section changed — reload does not apply it; restart required")
856		}
857		return w
858	}
859	
860	// reordered reports whether the paths common to BOTH slices appear in a different
861	// relative order in desired than in live. The common subsequence has the same length
862	// on both sides (it is the name intersection), so a positional mismatch is a reorder.
863	func reordered(live, desired []config.Path) bool {
864		desiredNames := make(map[string]struct{}, len(desired))
865		for _, p := range desired {
866			desiredNames[p.Name] = struct{}{}
867		}
868		liveNames := make(map[string]struct{}, len(live))
869		for _, p := range live {
870			liveNames[p.Name] = struct{}{}
871		}
872		var liveCommon, desiredCommon []string
873		for _, p := range live {
874			if _, ok := desiredNames[p.Name]; ok {
875				liveCommon = append(liveCommon, p.Name)
876			}
877		}
878		for _, p := range desired {
879			if _, ok := liveNames[p.Name]; ok {
880				desiredCommon = append(desiredCommon, p.Name)
881			}
882		}
883		for i := range liveCommon {
884			if liveCommon[i] != desiredCommon[i] {
885				return true
886			}
887		}
888		return false
889	}
890	
891	// runningConfig advances the running config to the membership the bind now serves:
892	// the survivors (live paths not removed, keeping their ORIGINAL parameters and order)
893	// followed by the added paths (their new parameters). Non-path fields are carried over
894	// unchanged — a membership-only reload does not re-apply them. It mirrors the m.defs
895	// order the bind rebuilds on a Close→Open (survivors-in-order, then appended adds).
896	func runningConfig(live *config.Config, add []config.Path, remove []string) *config.Config {
897		removeSet := make(map[string]struct{}, len(remove))
898		for _, n := range remove {
899			removeSet[n] = struct{}{}
900		}
901		paths := make([]config.Path, 0, len(live.Paths)+len(add))
902		for _, p := range live.Paths {
903			if _, gone := removeSet[p.Name]; !gone {
904				paths = append(paths, p)
905			}
906		}
907		paths = append(paths, add...)
908		running := *live
909		running.Paths = paths
910		return &running
911	}
912	
913	// diffPaths computes, by name, which desired paths are not yet live (to add) and
914	// which live paths are no longer desired (to remove). It is a pure function so the
915	// reload diff is unit-testable without a running engine.
916	func diffPaths(live []string, desired []config.Path) (add []config.Path, remove []string) {
917		liveSet := make(map[string]struct{}, len(live))
918		for _, n := range live {
919			liveSet[n] = struct{}{}
920		}
921		desiredSet := make(map[string]struct{}, len(desired))
922		for _, p := range desired {
923			desiredSet[p.Name] = struct{}{}
924			if _, ok := liveSet[p.Name]; !ok {
925				add = append(add, p)
926			}
927		}
928		for _, n := range live {
929			if _, ok := desiredSet[n]; !ok {
930				remove = append(remove, n)
931			}
932		}
933		return add, remove
934	}
935	
936	// defaultFailbackDwell is how long a recovered higher-priority path must stay up
937	// before egress fails BACK to it, damping flap-induced thrash (T15 hysteresis).
938	// Unlike the probe-cadence/liveness thresholds (which are the shared
939	// telemetry.Default* single source of truth, D16), the failback dwell is not part
940	// of the failover-recovery budget — failover to a backup is instant — so it stays a
941	// device-local constant.
942	const defaultFailbackDwell = 5 * time.Second
943	
944	// fecConfig maps the validated [fec] config block onto the fec.Config the multipath
945	// Bind consumes (T24), or returns nil when FEC is disabled so the Bind runs the
946	// datapath with no parity plane. The ratio was already range-checked in config
947	// validation; the Bind re-validates defensively.
948	func fecConfig(f config.FEC) *fec.Config {
949		if !f.Enabled {
950			return nil
951		}
952		return &fec.Config{
953			DataShards:   f.DataShards,
954			ParityShards: f.ParityShards,
955			Deadline:     f.Deadline,
956		}
957	}
958	
959	// adaptiveFECConfig maps the [fec] block onto the adaptive controller config the multipath
960	// Bind drives (T29), or returns nil when FEC is disabled or the block did not opt into
961	// adaptive mode — in which case the Bind runs the fixed-ratio plane (T24) unchanged. The
962	// controller uses the simulation-proven default control law (internal/adaptivefec,
963	// DefaultConfig), with only the group geometry pinned to the configured ratio: DataShards
964	// (K) is the FEC data_shards and MaxParity (the parity CEILING) is the FEC parity_shards,
965	// which the receiver's decoder is built at. The Bind re-validates and cross-checks these
966	// against the FEC config defensively.
967	func adaptiveFECConfig(f config.FEC) *adaptivefec.Config {
968		if !f.Enabled || !f.Adaptive {
969			return nil
970		}
971		c := adaptivefec.DefaultConfig()

11	// Acceptance thresholds (Q1). These are the single source of truth for the

15	const (
16		// P1RecoverySeconds bounds transparent-failover recovery: after the active
17		// WAN is killed, the surviving TCP flow must have throughput restored within
18		// this many seconds, with no connection reset.
19		P1RecoverySeconds = 3
20	
21		// P2BondedMinFraction is the minimum bonded throughput as a fraction of the
22		// sum of the two paths' individual throughputs, under saturating load.
23		P2BondedMinFraction = 0.85
24		// P2MeteredMaxByteFraction caps the metered (5G) path's share of total bytes
25		// while the primary (Starlink) path is healthy — data-thrift.
26		P2MeteredMaxByteFraction = 0.01
27	
28		// P3MinRecoveredFraction is the minimum fraction of lost DATA frames the RS
29		// FEC must recover without retransmit, at each injected loss rate.
30		P3MinRecoveredFraction = 0.95
31		// P3MaxOverheadFactor bounds measured FEC overhead as a multiple of the
32		// configured parity ratio.
33		P3MaxOverheadFactor = 2.0
34	
35		// P4ResidualLossMax is the maximum post-recovery residual loss under steady
36		// P4SteadyLossRate path loss with adaptive FEC engaged.
37		P4ResidualLossMax = 0.005
38		// P4SteadyLossRate is the steady per-path loss rate at which the P4 residual
39		// and overhead-vs-baseline comparisons are made.
40		P4SteadyLossRate = 0.05
41	)
42	
43	// P3InjectedLossRates are the uniform loss rates at which fixed-ratio FEC
44	// recovery and overhead are asserted (P3).
45	var P3InjectedLossRates = []float64{0.05, 0.15}
46	
47	// P4 adaptive-overhead acceptance is a comparison, not a scalar: for equal loss
48	// masking, adaptive total overhead bytes must be <= the P3 fixed-FEC baseline
49	// bytes. The comparison is performed in the P4 e2e test against a recorded
50	// baseline run rather than a constant here.
51	
52	// Per-path liveness detection (T13/T39). Path liveness is entirely ours — the inner
53	// WireGuard keepalive is per-peer, not per-path — so a blackholed uplink under a
54	// live peer is detected only by these probes. These are NOT independent constants:
55	// they are ALIASES of the daemon's telemetry.Default* timing, so the e2e asserts
56	// against the exact cadence the daemon runs. This is the D16 reconciliation — there
57	// is one source of truth (internal/telemetry), not two tables that drift apart.
58	// PLivenessDownAfter is the silence (no authenticated probe echo) after which an up
59	// path is marked down; PLivenessProbeInterval is the probe/Tick cadence; detection
60	// latency is therefore bounded by DownAfter plus roughly one interval, and the e2e
61	// asserts a blackholed path is marked down within PLivenessDetectBudget.
62	//
63	// Relationship to P1RecoverySeconds (the 3s failover-recovery budget). Killing the
64	// ACTIVE WAN must restore the surviving flow within P1RecoverySeconds in BOTH
65	// directions. Recovery in EACH direction is (that side marks the path down) +
66	// (its scheduler reroutes, a sub-ms data-structure update). Crucially, failover is
67	// BIDIRECTIONAL and the two ends detect INDEPENDENTLY: the edge must mark the path
68	// down to reroute egress, AND the concentrator must mark it down to reroute its
69	// replies (D15/D16 — the earlier note budgeted only the edge term). Both detection
70	// clocks start at the kill and run CONCURRENTLY, so end-to-end bidirectional
71	// recovery is governed by the SLOWER of the two ends, not their sum:
72	//
73	//	recovery ≈ max(edgeDetect, concDetect) + reroute
74	//	         ≈ (DownAfter + one interval) + reroute        [the two ends are symmetric]
75	//	         = 1.2s + 0.2s + sub-ms ≈ 1.4s                 [worst-case detect, strict-'>' Tick]
76	//	         < PLivenessFailoverBudget = 1.6s              [budget adds one interval of headroom]
77	//
78	// So the NO-LOAD analytical recovery FLOOR is the detect term, DownAfter + one
79	// interval = 1.4s (the strict-'>' Tick needs one full interval past DownAfter to
80	// fire) plus a sub-ms reroute. PLivenessFailoverBudget = DownAfter + TWO intervals =
81	// 1.6s deliberately carries ONE interval BEYOND that 1.4s detect as headroom — it is
82	// a bound with slack, not the detect floor itself. The remaining ~1.4s up to the 3s
83	// deadline (3.0s − 1.6s) is the margin for CPU-scheduling jitter under a saturating
84	// flow — the D15 tail. Two T39 changes
85	// bought that margin back after the tail was found at ~3.1s: DownAfter was tightened
86	// 1500ms→1200ms and the interval 250ms→200ms (detect floor 1.75s→1.4s) WITHOUT reducing the
87	// six-lost-echo false-down tolerance (the ratio is unchanged); and the bind now
88	// advances liveness off the always-scheduled receive path too (D15), so a starved
89	// probe-loop timer no longer delays the concentrator-side detect. The correctness
90	// invariant when retuning is PLivenessFailoverBudget < P1RecoverySeconds, and the
91	// operational target is that MEASURED recovery under saturating load stays below
92	// P1RecoverySeconds every run — TestP1Failover is the guard.
93	//
94	// NOTE PLivenessDetectBudget is deliberately LARGER than PLivenessFailoverBudget: it
95	// is the SINGLE-PATH blackhole ASSERTION DEADLINE for TestLivenessBlackhole (detect
96	// worst-case + harness slack so that pump-loop e2e is not flaky), NOT the
97	// failover-composition term.
98	const (
99		PLivenessProbeInterval = telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval
100		PLivenessDownAfter     = telemetry.DefaultDownAfter
101		PLivenessUpSuccesses   = telemetry.DefaultUpSuccesses
102		// PLivenessFailoverBudget is the analytical per-direction failover-recovery bound:
103		// the 1.4s worst-case detect (DownAfter + one interval, strict-'>' Tick) plus one
104		// interval of headroom, = DownAfter + 2 intervals = 1.6s (the sub-ms reroute is
105		// absorbed by that headroom). Both directions are symmetric and detect concurrently,
106		// so it bounds end-to-end BIDIRECTIONAL recovery. It MUST stay below P1RecoverySeconds
107		// (1.6s < 3s, a ~1.4s jitter margin); TestP1Failover asserts measured recovery against
108		// P1RecoverySeconds and reports its margin against this budget.
109		PLivenessFailoverBudget = PLivenessDownAfter + 2*PLivenessProbeInterval
110		// PLivenessDetectBudget is the single-path blackhole assertion deadline (harness
111		// slack added on top of the analytical detect), NOT the failover budget.
112		PLivenessDetectBudget = PLivenessDownAfter + 1500*time.Millisecond
113	)

110	// RecordEcho registers a successful, authenticated probe echo as a liveness
111	// heartbeat. Only echoes that are CONSECUTIVE — no more than DownAfter apart —
112	// accumulate toward Up; an echo that arrives after a longer silence starts the
113	// streak over, so UpAfterSuccesses stray echoes spread across arbitrary silence
114	// never bring a Down path Up. Once UpAfterSuccesses consecutive heartbeats
115	// accumulate, a Down path transitions Up.
116	func (l *Liveness) RecordEcho() {
117		now := l.clock.Now()
118		if l.haveGood && now.Sub(l.lastGood) > l.cfg.DownAfter {
119			l.goodStreak = 0 // silence broke the run of consecutive echoes
120		}
121		l.lastGood = now
122		l.haveGood = true
123		l.goodStreak++
124		if l.state == StateDown && l.goodStreak >= l.cfg.UpAfterSuccesses {
125			l.transition(StateUp, now)

129	// Tick re-evaluates liveness against the clock. An Up path with no heartbeat for
130	// longer than DownAfter transitions Down; a Down path that has gone silent past
131	// DownAfter has its partial up-streak reset, so recovery requires a fresh run of
132	// UpAfterSuccesses consecutive echoes rather than echoes accumulated before the
133	// silence. Call Tick at least as often as the probe interval so detection latency
134	// stays within DownAfter plus one interval.

136		now := l.clock.Now()

100	// with the path name so every transition record carries the per-path field.
101	func NewLiveness(pathName string, cfg LivenessConfig, clock Clock, logger log.Logger) *Liveness {
102		return &Liveness{

110	// RecordEcho registers a successful, authenticated probe echo as a liveness
111	// heartbeat. Only echoes that are CONSECUTIVE — no more than DownAfter apart —
112	// accumulate toward Up; an echo that arrives after a longer silence starts the
113	// streak over, so UpAfterSuccesses stray echoes spread across arbitrary silence
114	// never bring a Down path Up. Once UpAfterSuccesses consecutive heartbeats
115	// accumulate, a Down path transitions Up.
116	func (l *Liveness) RecordEcho() {
117		now := l.clock.Now()
118		if l.haveGood && now.Sub(l.lastGood) > l.cfg.DownAfter {
119			l.goodStreak = 0 // silence broke the run of consecutive echoes
120		}
121		l.lastGood = now
122		l.haveGood = true
123		l.goodStreak++
124		if l.state == StateDown && l.goodStreak >= l.cfg.UpAfterSuccesses {
125			l.transition(StateUp, now)
126		}
127	}
128	
129	// Tick re-evaluates liveness against the clock. An Up path with no heartbeat for
130	// longer than DownAfter transitions Down; a Down path that has gone silent past
131	// DownAfter has its partial up-streak reset, so recovery requires a fresh run of
132	// UpAfterSuccesses consecutive echoes rather than echoes accumulated before the
133	// silence. Call Tick at least as often as the probe interval so detection latency
134	// stays within DownAfter plus one interval.
135	func (l *Liveness) Tick() {
136		now := l.clock.Now()
137		if !l.haveGood {
138			return
139		}
140		stale := now.Sub(l.lastGood) > l.cfg.DownAfter
141		switch {
142		case l.state == StateUp && stale:
143			l.goodStreak = 0
144			l.transition(StateDown, now)
145		case l.state == StateDown && stale:
146			l.goodStreak = 0
147		}
148	}

151	func (l *Liveness) State() PathState { return l.state }

157			"from", from.String(),
158			"to", to.String(),
